Actinic keratoses.description: Actinic keratoses (AK) are rough, scaly patches on the skin caused by years of sun exposure. They are considered precancerous — if left untreated, a small percentage can develop into squamous cell carcinoma. They most commonly appear on sun-exposed areas like the face, scalp, ears, neck, and hands.
causes: Prolonged or repeated exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation from sunlight
Use of tanning beds or sunlamps
Fair skin, light hair, or light eyes (lower natural UV protection)
Weakened immune system (e.g., from medications or illness)
History of significant sunburns, especially in childhood
symptoms: Rough, dry, scaly patch of skin, usually less than 1 inch (2.5 cm) in diameter
Flat to slightly raised patch or bump on the top layer of skin
Hard, wart-like surface in some cases
Itching, burning, or tenderness in the affected area
Color ranging from pink to red to brown
whatToObserve: Whether the patch is growing in size or spreading to nearby areas
Any change in color — particularly darkening or developing multiple shades
Hardening, thickening, or crusting of the patch surface
New symptoms like bleeding, ulceration, or rapid growth (may indicate cancer)
recommendations: See a dermatologist promptly — treatment can prevent progression to skin cancer
Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen daily to all exposed skin
Wear protective clothing, wide-brimmed hats, and UV-blocking sunglasses outdoors
Avoid direct sun during peak hours (10 AM – 4 PM)
Do not pick or scratch the patch as this can cause infection or scarring
Follow up regularly with a dermatologist for monitoring and retreatment if needed
Actinic keratoses.whenToSeekCare: Seek urgent care if the patch bleeds spontaneously, grows rapidly, becomes very thick or hard, or develops an open sore — these signs may indicate cancer.

Basal cell carcinoma.description: Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) is the most common form of skin cancer, arising from basal cells in the deepest layer of the epidermis. While it rarely spreads to other parts of the body, it can cause significant local tissue damage if left untreated. It is highly curable when detected and treated early.
causes: Cumulative ultraviolet (UV) radiation exposure from sunlight over many years
History of severe or repeated sunburns, especially in childhood
Tanning bed use
Fair skin, freckles, or a tendency to burn rather than tan
Exposure to radiation therapy or certain chemicals (e.g., arsenic)
symptoms: A pearly or waxy bump, often on the face, ears, or neck
A flat, flesh-colored or brown scar-like lesion on the chest or back
A bleeding or scabbing sore that heals and then returns
Pink growth with raised edges and a crusted center
A white, waxy, scar-like lesion without a clearly defined border
whatToObserve: Any sore or spot that does not heal within 2–3 weeks
Changes in size, shape, or color of a skin lesion
A spot that bleeds easily when rubbed or bumped
Crusting, oozing, or formation of an open ulcer
recommendations: Consult a dermatologist or oncologist immediately for biopsy and treatment planning
Do not attempt to treat or remove the lesion yourself
Protect the area from further sun exposure using SPF 50+ sunscreen and clothing
Maintain a record of photos to track any changes before your appointment
Discuss treatment options: surgical excision, Mohs surgery, topical therapies, or radiation
Schedule regular full-body skin checks going forward
Basal cell carcinoma.whenToSeekCare: Seek immediate medical attention if the lesion bleeds heavily, grows rapidly, becomes painful, or you notice similar lesions appearing elsewhere.

Benign keratosis-like lesions.description: Benign keratosis-like lesions include seborrheic keratoses and similar non-cancerous growths. They appear as waxy, stuck-on-looking, wart-like patches that vary in color from light tan to dark brown or black. Though their appearance can be alarming, they are completely harmless and do not become cancerous.
causes: Natural aging process — extremely common in adults over 50
Genetic predisposition (often runs in families)
Unknown exact triggers, but not caused by sun exposure or viruses
Hormonal changes may encourage their development
symptoms: Waxy, scaly, slightly raised growth that looks pasted or stuck onto the skin
Color ranging from light tan, brown, to almost black
Round or oval shape, typically 1 cm to 3 cm in diameter
May itch occasionally, especially if irritated by clothing
Can appear singly or in clusters across the chest, back, shoulders, or face
whatToObserve: Rapid change in size or color (should grow slowly, if at all)
Development of bleeding, oozing, or persistent crusting
Any lesion that looks distinctly different from others (the 'ugly duckling' sign)
New symptoms like pain or tenderness, which are unusual for benign keratoses
recommendations: No treatment is necessary unless the lesion is irritated, itchy, or bothersome
Consult a dermatologist if you are unsure whether the growth is benign
Removal options (cryotherapy, laser, or curettage) are available for cosmetic reasons
Avoid picking, scratching, or scrubbing the area
Moisturize regularly to reduce itchiness
Benign keratosis-like lesions.whenToSeekCare: See a dermatologist if any lesion changes rapidly, bleeds, ulcerates, or if a new lesion looks markedly different from existing ones.

Chickenpox.description: Chickenpox is a highly contagious viral infection caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV). It produces a characteristic itchy, blister-like rash that spreads over the body. While usually mild in healthy children, it can cause serious complications in adults, newborns, pregnant women, and immunocompromised individuals.
causes: Infection with the varicella-zoster virus (VZV)
Direct contact with the fluid from chickenpox blisters
Breathing in respiratory droplets from an infected person
Contact with someone who has shingles (reactivated VZV)
Being unvaccinated or not previously infected
symptoms: Itchy, red rash that begins as small spots and develops into fluid-filled blisters
Rash typically starts on the chest, back, and face, then spreads all over the body
Fever, fatigue, loss of appetite, and headache (1–2 days before the rash)
Blisters that break open and crust over within a few days
New crops of blisters appearing over several days
whatToObserve: Signs of secondary bacterial infection: increasing redness, warmth, swelling, or pus
Rash involving the eyes — requires urgent attention
Difficulty breathing or chest pain
Severe headache, stiff neck, or confusion, which may indicate serious complications
recommendations: Stay home and isolate until all blisters have crusted over (typically 5–7 days after rash onset)
Avoid scratching — keep nails short and wear cotton mittens if needed
Use calamine lotion or antihistamines (as advised) to relieve itching
Take lukewarm oatmeal baths to soothe the skin
Stay well-hydrated and rest adequately
Contact a doctor if you are an adult, pregnant, immunocompromised, or symptoms worsen
Chickenpox.whenToSeekCare: Seek urgent care if you have difficulty breathing, confusion, a very high fever, a rash near the eyes, or signs of bacterial infection in blisters.

Cowpox.description: Cowpox is a rare viral skin infection caused by the cowpox virus, a member of the Orthopoxvirus family. It typically spreads to humans through direct contact with infected animals (cats, rodents, cattle). The infection produces distinctive lesions and is generally self-limiting, but it can be serious in immunocompromised individuals.
causes: Direct skin contact with an infected animal, most commonly cats or rodents
Less commonly, contact with infected cattle
The cowpox virus enters through breaks or cuts in the skin
Human-to-human transmission is very rare
symptoms: Red, inflamed spot at the site of infection that progresses to a blister within days
Blister fills with fluid, then pus, before crusting over (similar to a smallpox lesion)
Lesions are typically 1–3 on the hands, face, or arms
Surrounding skin becomes swollen and tender
Flu-like symptoms: fever, fatigue, swollen lymph nodes, and muscle aches
whatToObserve: Spreading of lesions to new areas of the body
Increasing redness, swelling, or pus suggesting secondary bacterial infection
Lesions near the eyes, which can cause serious eye damage
Systemic symptoms that worsen rather than improve over 2–3 weeks
recommendations: See a doctor to confirm the diagnosis — cowpox is rare and can be confused with other infections
Avoid touching or squeezing the lesions
Wash hands thoroughly and frequently to prevent spread
Keep the affected area clean and covered with a sterile dressing
Avoid contact with vulnerable individuals (infants, elderly, immunocompromised)
Do not apply over-the-counter creams without medical advice
Cowpox.whenToSeekCare: Seek prompt medical attention if lesions are near the eyes, if you are immunocompromised, or if you develop a high fever with systemic illness.

Dermatofibroma.description: Dermatofibromas are common, benign (non-cancerous) skin growths that develop in the deeper layers of the skin. They are firm, brownish nodules most often found on the legs. The exact cause is unknown, but they are harmless and rarely require treatment unless they are cosmetically bothersome or uncomfortable.
causes: Exact cause unknown; may develop in response to a minor injury (insect bite, splinter)
Overgrowth of fibrous tissue in the dermis
More common in women and in adults aged 20–40
Not caused by infection or sun exposure
symptoms: Small, firm bump under the skin surface, usually 0.5–1.5 cm in diameter
Color ranging from pink, tan, brown, or reddish-purple
Located most often on the lower legs, but can appear anywhere
Dimples inward when pinched (the 'dimple sign' — a key diagnostic feature)
Usually painless, though may be tender or itch occasionally
whatToObserve: Rapid growth in size over a short period
Change in color, especially darkening or becoming multicolored
Spontaneous bleeding or ulceration
Loss of the classic dimple sign (may warrant re-evaluation)
recommendations: No treatment is required if the lesion is not bothersome
Consult a dermatologist to confirm the diagnosis and rule out other conditions
Avoid repeatedly irritating or traumatizing the area
Surgical excision is available if the lesion causes pain, itching, or cosmetic concern
Do not attempt to remove it at home
Dermatofibroma.whenToSeekCare: See a dermatologist if the nodule grows rapidly, changes significantly in appearance, bleeds, or if you develop multiple new similar growths unexpectedly.

HFMD.description: Hand, Foot, and Mouth Disease (HFMD) is a common, contagious viral illness primarily affecting children under 5, though adults can also be infected. It is caused by enteroviruses (most commonly Coxsackievirus A16). The disease is generally mild and self-limiting, resolving within 7–10 days.
causes: Infection with enteroviruses, most commonly Coxsackievirus A16 or Enterovirus 71
Direct contact with an infected person's saliva, blister fluid, feces, or nasal secretions
Touching contaminated surfaces and then touching the face
Most contagious during the first week of illness
Common in childcare settings, schools, and crowded environments
symptoms: Fever, reduced appetite, sore throat, and general malaise (1–2 days before rash)
Painful sores or ulcers in the mouth (tongue, gums, inside cheeks)
Flat or raised red spots or blisters on the palms of the hands and soles of the feet
Rash may also appear on the buttocks, knees, and elbows
Children may refuse to eat or drink due to mouth pain
whatToObserve: Dehydration signs: dry mouth, no tears when crying, sunken eyes, reduced urination
High or persistent fever lasting more than 3 days
Rapid worsening of symptoms or unusual sleepiness/irritability
Nail loss or change (can occur several weeks after infection — usually temporary)
recommendations: Isolate the affected person from school, childcare, or work until fever-free and sores have healed
Offer cold fluids, ice cream, or yogurt to soothe mouth pain and maintain hydration
Use over-the-counter pain relievers (paracetamol/ibuprofen) for fever and pain as directed
Rinse mouth with warm salt water to ease sore mouth symptoms
Wash hands frequently with soap and water, especially after diaper changes
Disinfect frequently touched surfaces and objects
HFMD.whenToSeekCare: Seek urgent care if the child shows signs of dehydration, has a very high or persistent fever, is unusually drowsy or limp, or if symptoms worsen significantly after initial improvement.

Healthy.description: The AI analysis did not detect any significant skin condition in the submitted image. The skin appears healthy with no visible signs of a lesion, rash, or abnormality. While this is a reassuring result, it is not a substitute for a clinical examination by a qualified dermatologist.
symptoms: No concerning lesions or abnormalities detected in this image
Skin tone and texture appear within normal range
whatToObserve: Any new moles, spots, or growths that appear or change over time
Persistent itching, redness, or irritation in any area of the skin
Sores that do not heal within 2–3 weeks
Changes to existing moles using the ABCDE rule (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolving)
recommendations: Maintain a daily skincare routine with gentle cleansing and moisturizing
Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen every day, even when it is cloudy
Perform monthly self-examinations to stay familiar with your skin
Schedule an annual full-body skin check with a dermatologist
Stay hydrated and follow a balanced diet to support skin health
Avoid tanning beds and minimize peak-hours sun exposure
Healthy.whenToSeekCare: Consult a dermatologist if you notice any new or changing skin spots, persistent rashes, or lesions that itch, bleed, or do not heal.

Measles.description: Measles is a highly contagious viral infection caused by the measles virus (a paramyxovirus). It is characterised by a distinctive red rash that spreads from the face downwards. Measles is a major cause of childhood morbidity and mortality worldwide and can lead to serious complications including pneumonia, encephalitis, and death.
causes: Infection with the measles virus (Morbillivirus)
Breathing in respiratory droplets from an infected person's cough or sneeze
The virus can remain airborne in a room for up to 2 hours after an infected person leaves
Being unvaccinated or incompletely vaccinated (less than 2 doses of MMR vaccine)
Travel to or contact with individuals from areas with active outbreaks
symptoms: High fever (often above 40°C / 104°F)
Cough, runny nose, and red, watery eyes (conjunctivitis) — the '3 Cs'
Koplik's spots: tiny white spots inside the mouth, appearing 2–3 days before the rash
A red, blotchy rash that begins on the face and spreads downwards to the neck, trunk, arms, and legs
General malaise, loss of appetite, and lymph node swelling
whatToObserve: Difficulty breathing or fast breathing (may indicate pneumonia)
Severe headache, stiff neck, confusion, or seizures (may indicate encephalitis)
Ear pain or discharge (ear infection complication)
Signs of severe dehydration, particularly in young children
recommendations: Isolate immediately — measles is one of the most contagious diseases known
Contact a doctor or public health authority right away
Rest, stay hydrated, and take paracetamol/ibuprofen to manage fever (never aspirin in children)
Vitamin A supplementation is recommended for children with measles to reduce severity
Ensure all household contacts who are unvaccinated receive the MMR vaccine urgently
Report the case to local public health authorities as required by law in most regions
Measles.whenToSeekCare: Seek emergency care immediately if you or a child has difficulty breathing, seizures, confusion, loss of consciousness, or severe ear pain alongside a measles rash.

Melanocytic nevi.description: Melanocytic nevi, commonly known as moles, are benign growths formed by clusters of melanocytes (pigment-producing cells). Most people have between 10 and 40 moles. While the vast majority are completely harmless, regular monitoring is important as some moles can undergo changes and, rarely, develop into melanoma.
causes: Genetic predisposition — tendency to develop moles is inherited
UV radiation exposure, especially in childhood and adolescence, can trigger new moles
Hormonal changes (puberty, pregnancy) can cause moles to darken or enlarge slightly
Fair skin individuals tend to have more moles
symptoms: Small, round or oval brown, tan, or skin-colored spots on the skin
Usually flat or slightly raised with smooth, regular borders
Typically uniform in color and less than 6 mm in diameter
Generally stable in size and appearance over years
Usually painless; may rarely itch if irritated by clothing or jewellery
whatToObserve: Asymmetry: one half of the mole does not match the other
Border irregularity: edges are ragged, notched, or blurred
Color variation: multiple shades of brown, black, red, white, or blue within one mole
Diameter: the mole becomes larger than 6 mm (about the size of a pencil eraser)
Evolving: any change in size, shape, color, or a mole that starts to bleed or itch
recommendations: Perform a monthly skin self-examination in good lighting using a mirror
Use the ABCDE rule (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolving) to assess each mole
See a dermatologist annually for a professional full-body skin check
Photograph moles periodically to track any changes over time
Apply sunscreen daily and avoid peak UV hours to reduce further mole development
Report any concerning changes to a dermatologist promptly
Melanocytic nevi.whenToSeekCare: See a dermatologist immediately if a mole bleeds, itches, changes rapidly in size or shape, develops irregular borders, or looks strikingly different from your other moles.

Melanoma.description: Melanoma is the most serious and aggressive form of skin cancer, arising from melanocytes. Although it accounts for only about 1% of all skin cancers, it causes the majority of skin cancer deaths due to its tendency to metastasize to other organs. Early detection and treatment are critical — when caught early, melanoma is highly treatable; advanced melanoma is much harder to cure.
causes: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation from sunlight and tanning beds
History of severe, blistering sunburns — especially in childhood
Having many moles (more than 50) or atypical (dysplastic) moles
Personal or family history of melanoma
Fair skin, light hair, light eyes, and freckles
Weakened immune system
symptoms: A mole that changes in size, shape, or color
A lesion with irregular, ragged, or notched borders
A spot with multiple colors (brown, black, red, white, or blue)
A lesion larger than 6 mm (pencil eraser) or growing in size
A mole that itches, bleeds, oozes, or does not heal
whatToObserve: Any mole or skin spot that is changing, growing, or looks different from others
New spots that appear and do not look like any other spots on your body
Lesions that feel different: raised, bumpy, or tender
Swollen lymph nodes near the suspicious lesion
recommendations: See a dermatologist or oncologist URGENTLY — do not delay
Do not attempt any home treatment or removal
Avoid sun exposure on the lesion; cover it with a bandage if possible
Document the lesion with clear photographs before your appointment
Be prepared to discuss your family history of skin cancer and your sun exposure history
Ask about dermoscopy, biopsy, and sentinel lymph node procedures
Melanoma.whenToSeekCare: Seek emergency dermatology consultation immediately. Melanoma is time-sensitive — same-day or next-day assessment is strongly recommended if this diagnosis is suspected.

Monkeypox.description: Monkeypox (mpox) is a viral zoonotic disease caused by the monkeypox virus, an Orthopoxvirus. It causes a distinctive rash with lesions progressing through stages (macule → papule → vesicle → pustule → scab). Since 2022, it has been declared a global health emergency by the WHO due to widespread human-to-human transmission. Early identification and isolation are essential.
causes: Infection with the monkeypox virus (Orthopoxvirus genus)
Close physical contact with an infected person, including skin-to-skin contact
Contact with infected body fluids, lesion material, or respiratory secretions
Contact with contaminated bedding, clothing, or towels
Contact with infected animals (rodents, primates) in endemic regions
symptoms: Fever, headache, muscle aches, backache, swollen lymph nodes, and exhaustion
A distinctive rash appearing 1–5 days after fever onset
Lesions progressing through stages: flat spots → raised bumps → fluid-filled blisters → pustules → scabs
Lesions may appear on the face, hands, feet, genitals, mouth, and body
Lesions at the same stage of development (unlike chickenpox where stages differ)
whatToObserve: Rapid spread of lesions to new areas of the body
Lesions involving the eyes (can cause vision loss)
Difficulty swallowing or breathing if lesions develop in the throat
Signs of secondary bacterial infection: excessive redness, swelling, or pus
recommendations: Isolate immediately and contact your doctor or public health authority
Do not attend work, school, or public places until all scabs have fallen off
Cover all lesions with clothing or bandages to prevent spread
Avoid close contact, shared items, and sexual contact until fully recovered
Wear a mask when around others, particularly in enclosed spaces
Report to local health authorities — mpox is a notifiable disease in most countries
Monkeypox.whenToSeekCare: Seek urgent medical care if you have lesions near the eyes or genitals, difficulty breathing or swallowing, high fever with rapid worsening, or any signs of severe systemic illness.

Squamous cell carcinoma.description: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) is the second most common form of skin cancer, arising from squamous cells in the outer layers of the skin. Unlike basal cell carcinoma, SCC has a greater tendency to spread (metastasize) to lymph nodes and distant organs if not treated promptly. Early treatment offers a very high cure rate.
causes: Cumulative UV radiation exposure from sun and tanning beds
History of precancerous lesions such as actinic keratoses
Chronic skin inflammation, scarring, or long-standing wounds
Infection with certain strains of human papillomavirus (HPV)
Weakened immune system (e.g., organ transplant recipients, HIV)
Exposure to carcinogens such as arsenic, radiation, or industrial chemicals
symptoms: A firm, red nodule on sun-exposed skin (face, ears, lips, hands)
A flat lesion with a scaly, crusted surface
A new sore or raised area on an old scar or ulcer
A rough, scaly patch on the lip that may become an open sore
A red sore or rough patch inside the mouth or on the genitals
whatToObserve: Any sore that does not heal within 3 weeks
Growth, hardening, or crusting of an existing skin lesion
Swollen, firm lymph nodes near the lesion
Numbness, pain, or weakness around the lesion (may indicate nerve involvement)
recommendations: See a dermatologist or oncologist urgently for biopsy and staging
Do not delay — SCC can spread if treatment is postponed
Protect the lesion from further UV damage with high-SPF sunscreen and clothing
Document the lesion with photographs and note any growth or changes
Discuss treatment options: surgical excision, Mohs micrographic surgery, radiation, or immunotherapy
Follow up closely after treatment — SCC has a significant recurrence rate
Squamous cell carcinoma.whenToSeekCare: Seek urgent specialist care — do not delay more than a few days. Swollen lymph nodes near the lesion require immediate evaluation as they may indicate metastasis.

Vascular lesions.description: Vascular lesions are abnormalities of blood vessels visible on or near the skin surface. They encompass a wide variety of conditions including cherry angiomas, spider angiomas, port-wine stains, hemangiomas, and telangiectasias. Most are benign and harmless, though their appearance can be a concern, and in some cases they may indicate an underlying systemic condition.
causes: Genetic or developmental abnormalities in blood vessel formation
Aging — cherry angiomas and spider veins become more common with age
Sun damage causing superficial telangiectasias
Hormonal changes during pregnancy or due to birth control pills
Liver disease can cause spider angiomas (multiple may signal a problem)
Trauma or injury causing localized blood vessel overgrowth
symptoms: Small, bright red dome-shaped dots (cherry angiomas) — very common and harmless
Spider-like vessels radiating from a central point (spider angiomas)
Flat, deep red or purple birthmark-like patches (port-wine stains)
Bluish or reddish raised growths (hemangiomas), particularly in infants
Fine, dilated red or purple lines on the face or legs (telangiectasias)
whatToObserve: Rapid increase in the number or size of vascular lesions
Bleeding from a lesion that does not stop with gentle pressure
Multiple new spider angiomas appearing suddenly — may indicate liver disease
A lesion that changes color, becomes raised, or shows signs of ulceration
recommendations: Consult a dermatologist to confirm the type of vascular lesion
Most require no treatment — monitoring is sufficient for benign lesions
Laser therapy, sclerotherapy, or other procedures can remove lesions for cosmetic reasons
If multiple spider angiomas appear suddenly, ask your doctor to check liver function
Apply sunscreen daily to prevent UV-related telangiectasias from worsening
Avoid heat and alcohol, which can dilate blood vessels and worsen redness
Vascular lesions.whenToSeekCare: See a doctor if a vascular lesion bleeds significantly, grows rapidly, becomes painful, or if you develop a sudden crop of new lesions — particularly spider angiomas — which can signal an underlying medical condition.
